title: Denying a Refund
content:
Overview


Refund denials are among the most difficult conversations agents will handle. The customer may be frustrated, disappointed, or angry. Your goal is to communicate the decision clearly while preserving the relationship and steering them toward a positive outcome with their remaining credits.



Before You Deny — Verify First


Always confirm the denial is correct before communicating it. Check:


  • Has it been more than 72 hours since purchase?
  • Have any sessions been booked (even if not completed)?
  • Have any credits been used?

If the answer to any of these is yes, the refund is not eligible per policy. Refer to the Refund Policy article for full details.



How to Communicate the Denial


Step 1 — Acknowledge their frustration:


"I completely understand why you'd want a refund, and I'm sorry for the inconvenience. Let me explain what's happening with your account."


Step 2 — State the reason clearly without being cold:


  • If outside the 72-hour window: "Our refund window is 72 hours from the date of purchase, and unfortunately, your purchase falls outside that timeframe. I know that's not what you were hoping to hear."
  • If sessions were booked: "Because a session from your package has already been scheduled, our policy doesn't allow for a refund. The credit has been allocated to that booking."
  • If credits were used: "Since some of your credits have already been used for completed sessions, we're unable to process a refund for this package."

Step 3 — Redirect to the positive:


"The good news is your remaining credits are still valid for six months from the purchase date, and you can use them with any tutor on the platform. So the value isn't lost — it's waiting for you when you're ready."


Step 4 — Offer an alternative path:


"Would you like me to help you schedule a session with one of our tutors? I can also check if there are any promotions or rewards you might be eligible for."



If the Customer Insists or Escalates


Do not argue. If the customer continues to push after you've explained the policy:


"I completely understand your position. Let me document your concern and escalate it to our Lead for review. They'll reach out within 24 hours with a final decision. That way you'll have a definitive answer from the right person."


Create a ticket under Billing category, document the situation, and escalate. Never say "I'll approve this" or make promises on behalf of the Lead.



Key Phrases


  • "I wish I could give you a different answer, but our policy is in place to be fair to all our students and tutors."
  • "Your credits are still active and can be used anytime in the next [X] months."
  • "I've documented everything, and our Lead will follow up with you directly."
  • Never say: "I agree with you, but..." — this undermines the policy and creates false hope.
